question: Explain the purpose and function of the Program Status Register (PSR) in ARM microcontrollers. List the four condition flags and their meanings.
expectedPoints: PSR contains processor state including condition flags, mode bits, interrupt enable bits
N (Negative) flag: set when result is negative (signed)
Z (Zero) flag: set when result is zero
C (Carry) flag: set when unsigned carry/borrow occurs
V (Overflow) flag: set when signed overflow occurs

question: Differentiate between CPSR and SPSR. When is SPSR used?
expectedPoints: CPSR = Current PSR holds current processor state at any time
SPSR = Saved PSR saves CPSR value when exception is taken
SPSR is automatically loaded with CPSR on exception entry
SPSR is restored to CPSR on exception return (using SUBS PC,LR,#4 or similar)
Only one SPSR exists per privilege mode

question: Explain the difference between CMP, CMN, TST, and TEQ instructions. For each instruction, mention the operation performed and state which flags are affected.
expectedPoints: CMP: Subtracts operand from register, sets NZCV, result discarded
CMN: Adds operand to register, sets NZCV, result discarded
TST: Bitwise AND of operands, sets N,Z,C flags (C cleared), result discarded
TEQ: Bitwise XOR of operands, sets N,Z,C flags (C unchanged), result discarded
All are compare/test instructions - they set flags without storing result

question: Write ARM assembly code to check if bit 4 of register R0 is set. Explain the instructions used.
expectedPoints: Use TST instruction: TST R0, #0x10 (or #16)
TST performs AND operation between R0 and mask value
If bit is set, result is non-zero, Z flag is cleared
Use conditional branch: BNE (branch if Z=0) or BEQ (branch if Z=1)
Explain: 0x10 = 0b00010000 tests only bit 4

question: Explain the MRS and MSR instructions. Give examples of how to read and write to CPSR fields.
expectedPoints: MRS Rd, CPSR - copies CPSR to Rd (read flags/mode)
MSR CPSR_all, Rd - writes entire CPSR from Rd
MSR CPSR_flg, Rd - writes only flag bits (bits 31-28)
MSR CPSR_ctl, Rd - writes only control bits (mode, IRQ/FIQ disable)
Used for saving/restoring processor state, changing processor mode

question: List at least 8 ARM condition codes with their meanings and the flag states required for execution.
expectedPoints: EQ - Equal (Z=1), NE - Not Equal (Z=0)
CS/HS - Carry Set/Higher or Same (C=1), CC/LO - Carry Clear/Lower (C=0)
MI - Minus (N=1), PL - Plus (N=0)
VS - Overflow Set (V=1), VC - Overflow Clear (V=0)
GT - Greater Than (Z=0 and N=V), LT - Less Than (N!=V)
GE - Greater or Equal (N=V), LE - Less or Equal (Z=1 or N!=V)
AL - Always (always executes)

question: Explain the signed and unsigned comparison operations in ARM. How are the flags used to determine greater than, less than, and equal conditions for both signed and unsigned numbers?
expectedPoints: For unsigned comparison: HI (C=1 and Z=0), LS (C=0 or Z=1), HS/CS (C=1), LO/CC (C=0)
For signed comparison: GT uses (Z=0 and N=V), LT uses (N!=V), GE uses (N=V), LE uses (Z=1 or N!=V)
C flag for unsigned: indicates carry out (addition) or borrow (subtraction)
V flag for signed: indicates overflow when signs don't match expected
Z flag for equality in both signed and unsigned
N flag indicates sign of result for signed operations
Example: CMP R0,R1 with R0=5,R1=10: 5-10=-5 (N=1,V=0 if no overflow), so R0<R1

question: Write an ARM assembly program to find the largest of three numbers stored in memory locations. Explain the logic and use of conditional instructions.
expectedPoints: Load three values from memory into registers (e.g., LDR R0, [R1], LDR R2, [R3], LDR R4, [R5])
Use CMP to compare first two numbers: CMP R0,R2
Use conditional move: MOVGT R6,R0 (if R0>R2) else MOVLE R2
Compare result with third number: CMP R6,R4
Use conditional move again to get largest: MOVGT R7,R6, MOVLE R7,R4
Explain each comparison sets flags, conditional execution saves branches
Alternatively use BGT/BLT conditional branch labels
Explain that GT/LT check N and V flags together for signed comparison

question: Explain how exceptions are handled in ARM with respect to PSR. Describe the automatic saving and restoring of PSR and the role of SPSR.
expectedPoints: When exception occurs: CPSR is automatically copied to SPSR of target mode
Processor mode changes to corresponding exception mode (SVC, IRQ, FIQ, etc.)
PC is saved to LR (link register) with offset (e.g., LR-4 for IRQ)
CPSR mode bits change to exception mode, interrupts may be disabled automatically
To return from exception: use SUBS PC,LR,#4 (or appropriate offset) for IRQ
This instruction restores SPSR to CPSR and moves LR to PC
Explain example: IRQ exception saves CPSR to SPSR_irq, PC to LR_irq, enters IRQ mode
On return: SUBS PC,LR,#4 restores SPSR to CPSR, resumes interrupted code

question: Design an ARM subroutine that checks whether a number is positive, negative, or zero. Explain the use of flag-checking conditional instructions.
expectedPoints: Use CMP R0,#0 to compare number with zero
This sets N flag if R0<0 (negative), Z flag if R0=0, clears both if positive
Use conditional branches based on flags
BEQ Zero (branch if Z=1) - number is zero
BMI Negative (branch if N=1) - number is negative
BPL Positive (branch if N=0 and Z=0) - number is positive
Alternative using flags directly: check Z for zero, then N for negative, else positive
Explain each conditional checks specific flag combinations
Return codes in R0: 0=zero, 1=positive, 2=negative
